coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated graphite foam as ultra-high active free-standing electrode for electrochemical hydrogen evolution and phenol degradation
coredata.prism:issueName: Advances in Nanomaterials for Catalysis and Electrochemical Processes
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Xiaomeng
Zhu, Yuanzhi
Han, Weiwei
Fan, Xiaobin
Li, Yang
Zhang, Fengbao
Zhang, Guoliang
Peng, Wenchao
Wang, Shaobin
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs, ∼5 nm) were synthesized and supported on graphite foam (GF) by a facile hydrothermal route. The obtained N-GQDs/GF composite demonstrates as an ultra-active free-standing electrode for electrochemical hydrogen evolution and activation of peroxymonosulfate to degrade phenol pollutant. An overpotential in the electrochemical hydrogen evolution is only −72 mV vs. RHE at 10 mA/cm2 with a Tafel slope of 84 mV/Dec. The N-GQDs/GF electrode can also achieve 100% phenol removal in short time in electrochemical degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
N-doping
Graphite foam
Electrochemical hydrogen evolution
Peroxymonosulfate activation
Degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM image of GF (a) and N-GQDs/GF with different magnifications (b, c); the inset of c shows the lattice space distance of N-GQDs; selected TEM image of N-GQDs/GF (d) and its corresponding elemental mapping images (d–f).
XPS survey spectrum (a) and high-resolution scans of N (b), and C (c) of N-GQDs/GF.

(a) Polarization curves of GF, N-GQDs, N-GQDs/GF, and Pt electrodes; (b) Tafel plots of different catalysts derived from polarization curves; (c) stability test for the N-GQDs/GF electrode; (d) EIS for the obtained electrodes; (f) phenol removal under various conditions.
Synthesis process of N-GQDs/GF electrode and the mechanism for HER and electrochemically assisted phenol degradation.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated graphite foam as ultra-high active free-standing electrode for electrochemical hydrogen evolution and phenol degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300350, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Curtin University, GPO Box U1987, Perth, WA 6845, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs, ∼5 nm) were synthesized and supported on graphite foam (GF) by a facile hydrothermal route. The obtained N-GQDs/GF composite demonstrates as an ultra-active free-standing electrode for electrochemical hydrogen evolution and activation of peroxymonosulfate to degrade phenol pollutant. An overpotential in the electrochemical hydrogen evolution is only −72 mV vs. RHE at 10 mA/cm2 with a Tafel slope of 84 mV/Dec. The N-GQDs/GF electrode can also achieve 100% phenol removal in short time in electrochemical degradation.

ce:section.ce:para: Fossil fuel depletion and environmental pollution are the two most serious problems nowadays (Gupta et al., 2014; Li et al., 2017; Saravanan et al., 2013a, 2015b). Hydrogen is a potential candidate, and electro-catalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) is an effective method for the production of hydrogen. Efficient catalysts for HER with lower overpotentials and high efficiencies are needed for large-scale applications (Li et al., 2011). Currently, Pt-group metals are good HER catalysts but limited by their rarity and expensiveness (Sau et al., 2010; Zheng et al., 2016).

Environmental pollution, especially the toxic wastewater has brought in great threats to our lives (Ali et al., 2012; Ali and Jain, 2004; Asfaram et al., 2015; Ghaedi et al., 2015; Saravanan et al., 2013b, 2015a, 2013c). Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) using peroxymonosulfate (PMS) as an oxidant could degrade the organic pollutants effectively (Hussain et al., 2013; Karthikeyan et al., 2012). The PMS could be activated by heterogeneous catalysts to release sulfate radicals. This process could also be achieved by electrolysis on a boron-doped diamond (BDD) anode for the degradation of organics (Song et al., 2018).

Recently, graphene (GR) based materials have shown great potential in both HER and PMS activation (Navalon et al., 2014; Robati et al., 2016). After doping with foreign non-metal atoms, the HER activity of GR could be enhanced due to the tuning of Gibbs free energy of H∗ adsorption (Ito et al., 2015). In addition, N-doping can induce nonradical oxidation in PMS activation, thus can increase the catalytic activity for organics degradation (Duan et al., 2015a; Sun et al., 2013). In this study, we synthesized an N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) decorated 3D graphite foam (N-GQDs/GF) composite by a facile hydrothermal route. The obtained N-GQDs/GF composite was free-standing and metal free, which is ultra-active for HER with a very small overpotential of −72 mV at 10 mA cm−1. Furthermore, it can activate the PMS effectively to degrade phenol under electrochemical conditions.

GF was synthesized with Ni foam as a template and CH4 as a carbon source. After etching of the Ni template, GF was in situ coated by N-GQDs using a facile hydrothermal method. Figs. 1 a and S1 show the SEM images of GF and N-GQDs/GF composite. No obvious differences could be observed, indicating that the 3D porous structre was well kept after the thermal treatment. The size of N-GQDs could be tuned by changing the amount of a surfactant (polyvinylpyrrolidone, PVP) (TEM, Fig. S1). The typical TEM image clearly shows the N-GQDs in 5 nm were uniformly immobilized on the GF surface (Figs. 1b and S2). The N-GQDs are highly graphitic with a lattice spacing distance of 0.24 nm, which is similar to those of graphite (1120) facet (Qu et al., 2013). Raman spectra (Fig. S4) of N-GQDs further show the stronger characteristic G-band (IG) of the graphitic carbon with respect to disordered D-band intensity (ID), indicating the presence of abundant carbon defects (Jia et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2016). The successful preparation of N-GQDs/GF composite is further supported by EELS elemental-mapping in Fig. 1d–f. Different from the widely distributed C signal on the whole GF support, the N signal can be clearly observed on the N-GQDs. Fig. S5 shows the XRD patterns for GF and N-GQDs/GF, and no differences could be found due to the low percentage and weak response of N-GQDs (Inset of Fig. S5).

Fig. 2 a shows XPS survey of the N-GQDs/GF, and the presence of C, O and N elements could be confirmed. Since GF was difficult to be doped with foreign atoms, the N should be in situ doped in GQDs (Ji et al., 2015). Deconvolution of the C 1 s peak (Fig. 2c) indicates three types of C, corresponding to sp2 C in graphene at 284.4 eV, sp3 C in CN and CO at 285.6 eV, and sp3 C in CO at 288.4 eV from carbonyls and carboxylates. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum of the N-GQDs/GF shows two peaks at 399.8 and 401.7 eV, attributing to the pyrrolic N (CNC) and graphitic N or NH bands, respectively.

Electrochemical HER activity of the N-GQDs/GF electrodes was then investigated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the unanchored N-GQDs and naked GF are inert. After the decoration with N-GQDs, an enhanced HER activity could be obtained on GF. Adjusting the PVP amount could affect the N-GQDs diameter (Fig. S3) and their HER activity (Fig. S6). The optimized N-GQDs/GF could achieve an overpotential of −72 mV at 10 mA cm−1, better than the reported free-standing electrodes (Table S1). The corresponding Tafel slope of N-GQDs/GF is 84 mV/dec, smaller than that of GF (237 mV/dec) and N-GQDs (104 mV/dec) (Fig. 3b). This Tafel slope value indicates a combined Volmer–Heyrovsky mechanism, and both the discharge reaction and the hydrogen adsorption influence the HER rate (Morales-Guio et al., 2014). The stability of the optimized N-GQDs/GF electrode was also investigated, and no deactivation can be observed even after 1000 cycles (Fig. 3c). In alkaline and neutral media, the N-GQDs/GF electrode also shows good performance with early overpotentials and small Tafel slopes (Fig. S7).

To further understand the mechanism of the enhanced activity, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra for these electrodes were investigated. As shown in Fig. 3d, the semicircle is mainly ascribed to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The Rct of unsupported N-GQDs is 7.2 Ω, while the Rct can be decreased to only 3 Ω by anchoring them on the surface of GF, attributing to the good electric conductivity of GF (1.8 Ω). The deceased resistance also implies the intimate contact between the GF and N-GQDs, which is convenient for the electron transfer.

In our previous work, N-doped carbon materials were proved to be good metal-free catalysts for the activation of PMS, which could be used for the degradation of organic pollutants (Duan et al., 2015b). Therefore, we also investigated the catalytic activity of these carbon samples for the electro activation of PMS. Phenol was selected as a model pollutant. As shown in Fig. 3e, without catalysts, PMS could hardly be activated for phenol degradation. GF was also an inert catalyst for this reaction. However, after decorated with N-GQDs, the N-GQDs/GF shows a good activity for PMS activation, and ∼80% phenol could be degraded in 90 min. After that, we also investigated the effect of electricity for this activation process. The activity of pure GF was slightly enhanced under a CV sweep. As for the N-GQDs/GF, the activity was increased greatly and 100% of phenol can be degraded in 50 min.

Fig. 4 shows the synthesis process and the proposed mechanism for HER and electrochemical PMS activation. During the HER process, the electrons could be transferred rapidly through the GF and accepted by the N-GQDs. The edges of N-GQDs as well as the carbon defects could act as the active sites for the generation of H2 (Route I) (Jia et al., 2016; Yan et al., 2016). As we reported previously, PMS could be activated by accepting or losing electrons to generate sulfate radicals (
SO
5
• -
or
SO
4
• -
), which could degrade phenol effectively (Duan et al., 2015b). This activation process could be realized on the N-GQDs, and could be enhanced under CV condition (Route II).

In summary, we have demonstrated a simple hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of N-GQDs/GF, which shows an ultra-high activity in HER with a very early overpotential of 72 mV and a small Tafel slope of 84 mV/dec. Furthermore, under CV condition, the N-GQDs/GF electrode is also effective to enhance the activation of PMS to release sulfate radicals, which could degrade the phenol pollutant effectively.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by a project (No. 21506158) from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) and a discovery project (DP150103026) from the Australian Research Council.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2018.04.072.
